elements.sent-2411-005: In this paper, we develop the structure theory of weights in the variable exponent setting by proving a version of the reverse H\"older inequality for the class of $\calA_\pp$ weights.
elements.sent-2411-006: Before stating our main theorem, we sketch earlier results to provide some context.
elements.sent-2411-007: The study of $A_p$ weights dates back to the early 1970s when Muckenhoupt \cite{MR0293384 proved the Hardy-Littlewood maximal operator is bounded on $L^p(v)$ if and only if $v \in A_p$.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Given $1< p < \infty$, a weight $v$ is a (scalar) $A_p$ weight if \begin{equation}[v]_{A_p} : = \sup_Q \dashint_Q v(x)\, dx \left( \dashint_Q v(y)^{1-p'}\, dy \right)^{p-1}<\infty,\end{equation} where the supremum is taken over all cubes $Q\subset \R^n$.
elements.sent-2411-009: A rich structural theory for these weights developed quickly (see \cite{duoandikoetxea_fourier_2000, MR0736522,MR0807149 ).}
elements.sent-2411-010: The fine properties of $A_p$ weights play an important role in many of their applications.
elements.sent-2411-011: Of particular interest is the reverse H\"older inequality, introduced by Coifman and Fefferman \cite{coifman_weighted_1974 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: They used it to give a simpler proof that the Hardy-Littlewood maximal operator is bounded, and to prove that singular integral operators are bounded on $L^p(v)$ for $v\in A_p$.
elements.sent-2411-013: They showed that given a weight $v\in A_p$, there exist constants $C$ and $r>1$ such that for all cubes $Q$, \begin{equation}\dashint_Q v(x)^r\, dx \leq C \left( \dashint_Q v(x)\, dx \right)^r.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-014: This is called a reverse H\"older inequality since the opposite inequality is just a normalized version of H\"older's inequality.
elements.sent-2411-015: By H\"older's inequality, it follows at once from the definition that the $A_p$ classes are nested: $A_p \subset A_q$ for all $q >p$.
elements.sent-2411-016: Moreover, as a consequence of the reverse H\"older inequality, they are left-open: if $v\in A_p$, then $v \in A_{p-\epsilon}$ for some $\epsilon>0$.
elements.sent-2411-017: To generalize $A_p$ weights to the variable Lebesgue spaces we need to define an alternative version of $A_p$, which we denote by $\calA_p$.
elements.sent-2411-018: Given $1 < p <\infty$, a weight $w$ is a scalar $\calA_p$ weight if \begin{equation}[w]_{\calA_p} : = \sup_Q \left( \dashint_Q w(x)^p\, dx \right)^{\frac{1}{p}} \left( \dashint_Q w(y)^{-p'}\, dy\right)^{\frac{1}{p'}}<\infty.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-019: Notice that $w \in \calA_p$ if and only if $v=w^p \in A_p$ with $[w]_{\calA_p =[v]_{A_p ^{\frac{1 {p }$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: The definition of classical $A_p$ weights is based on viewing the weight $v$ as a measure in the $L^p(v)$ norm, i.e., defining \begin{equation}\|f\|_{L^p(v)} = \left( \int_{\R^n} |f(x)|^p\, v(x) dx \right)^{\frac{1}{p}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-021: On the other hand, the definition of $\calA_p$ is based on viewing the weight $w=v^{\frac{1 {p }$ as a multiplier, i.e., we define the $L^p(w)$ norm by \begin{equation}\|f\|_{L^p(w)}= \|wf\|_{L^p(\R^n)} = \left( \int_{\R^n} |w(x) f(x)|^p\, dx \right)^{\frac{1}{p}}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: This approach of using weights as multipliers was first adopted by Muckenhoupt and Wheeden~\cite{MR340523 to define the ``off-diagonal" $A_{p,q}$ weights used with fractional integral operators.}
elements.sent-2411-023: One consequence of this approach is that the ``duality" of the weights has a much more symmetric expression: with this definition, $w\in \calA_p$ if and only if $w^{-1}\in \calA_{p'}$, whereas in the classical case, $v\in A_p$ if and only if $v^{1-p' =v^{-p'/p}\in A_{p'}$.}
elements.sent-2411-024: The $\calA_p$ weights also satisfy a reverse H\"older inequality: this follows at once from the classical reverse H\"older applied to the weight $v=w^p$: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:calAp-holder} \left( \dashint_Q w(x)^{rp}\, dx \right)^\frac{1}{rp} \leq C_p \left( \dashint_Q w(x)^p\, dx \right)^\frac{1}{p}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-025: Since $w \in \calA_p$ and $w^{-1}\in \calA_{p'}$ we can use this inequality to show that the class $\calA_p$ is both right and left open: there exists $\epsilon>0$ such that $w\in \calA_{p-\epsilon}$ and $w\in \calA_{p+\epsilon}$.

elements.sent-2411-140: In this section we define the scalar $\calA_\pp$ weights and prove a number of important, quantitative lemmas about them.

elements.sent-2411-324: Many of these definitions and results are drawn from our previous paper~\cite{ConvOpsOnVLS , and we refer the reader there for additional information.}
elements.sent-2411-325: Let $\calS_d$ denote the collection of $d\times d$, self-adjoint, positive semi-definite matrices.
elements.sent-2411-326: The operator norm of a matrix $W$ is given by \begin{equation}|W|_{\op} = \sup_{\substack{\ve\in \R^d |\ve|=1}} |W\ve|.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-327: The following lemmas are very useful for estimating operator norms.
elements.sent-2411-328: Let $U$ and $V$ be self-adjoint $d\times d$ matrices.
elements.sent-2411-330: A matrix weight is a measurable matrix function $W: \R^n \to \calS_d$ such that $|W(\cdot)|_{\op \in L^1_{\loc (\R^n)$, or equivalently, the eigenvalues of $W$ are locally integrable functions.}}
elements.sent-2411-331: A matrix weight is invertible if it is positive definite almost everywhere, or equivalently, all its eigenvalues are positive almost everywhere.
elements.sent-2411-332: Note that when $d=1$, matrix weights are simply locally integrable scalar weights.
elements.sent-2411-333: We now define the variable Lebesgue spaces for the vector-valued function setting.
elements.sent-2411-334: Given $\pp \in \Pp(\R^n)$, define $\Lpp(\R^n;\R^d)$ to be the collection of Lebesgue measurable functions $\vf : \R^n\to \R^d$ such that \begin{equation}\| \vf\|_{\Lpp(\R^n;\R^d)} : = \| |\vf|\|_{\Lpp(\R^n)} <\infty.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-335: Given a matrix weight $W:\R^n\to \calS_d$, define $\Lpp(W)$ to be the collection of Lebesgue measurable functions $\vf:\R^n\to \R^d$ such that \begin{equation}\| \vf\|_{\Lpp(W)} : = \| W\vf\|_{\Lpp(\R^n;\R^d)} <\infty.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-336: We are interested in the class matrix $\calA_\pp$, which generalizes both the definition of constant exponent matrix $\calA_p$ and the definition of scalar $\calA_\pp$ in~\cite{MR2927495, MR2837636 .}
elements.sent-2411-337: Given $\pp \in \Pp(\R^n)$ and an invertible matrix weight $W : \R^n\to \calS_d$, $W$ is a matrix $\calA_\pp$ weight, denoted $W \in \calA_\pp$, if \begin{equation}[W]_{\calA_\pp} : = \sup_Q |Q|^{-1}\big\| \big\| |W(x)W^{-1}(y)|_{\op} \chi_Q(y)\big\|_{\Lcpp_y(\R^n)} \chi_Q(x)\big\|_{\Lpp_x(\R^n)}<\infty.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-338: Our definition of $\calA_\pp$ if $\pp=p$ is constant reduces to the Roudenko definition (see~\cite{roudenko_matrix-weighted_2002 ) if we make the change of variables $W\mapsto V^{1/p}$.}
elements.sent-2411-340: First, this definition of $\calA_\pp$ has a very simple duality: $W \in \calA_\pp$ if and only if $W^{-1}\in \calA_\cpp$.
